confirmations.reason: Workspace Rules are stored in .agents/rules at the workspace or git root — a project-scoped file giving the agent standing instructions and context. This directly matches the capability.

Plugins are described as namespaced bundles grouping skills, rules, MCP servers, and hooks into a single package — directly matching the capability of bundling reusable capabilities/instructions the agent can load.

The feature explicitly describes hooks that run custom local shell scripts or commands at specific lifecycle points during execution, directly matching the capability of running custom logic on agent events.

Rules provide persistent constraints and reusable context, but they do not specifically shape the system prompt, persona, or output style. Rules are behavioral constraints, not output/system-prompt customization mechanisms.
The feature explicitly lets users choose from multiple leading frontier reasoning models to power the agent's core reasoning, directly realizing model selection.
MCP Integration describes support for the Model Context Protocol to connect the editor to local tools, databases, and services — directly realizing the external tool/connector protocol capability.
The built-in MCP server manager for integrating external context providers directly matches the capability of installing, configuring, scoping, and discovering MCP servers.
The feature explicitly describes OAuth authentication for MCP servers with browser/console authorization flows, directly realizing MCP authentication.
MCP Permission is described as a permission action governing agent access to MCP tools across local and remote MCP servers, directly matching tool allow-listing/governance for MCP.
The Memory feature is described as a searchable, cross-session knowledge store that agents can query to recall information beyond the current context — directly realizing persistent agent memory.
Context Compaction is explicitly described as reducing running context by summarizing older session history, directly realizing the context compaction capability.
Subagents are described as autonomous agents to which the primary agent delegates long-running operations in parallel — directly matching the spawn-focused sub-agents capability.
Multi-Agent Teamwork launches a collaborative multi-agent framework where a high-level goal is managed across multiple agents, directly realizing coordination of multiple communicating agents.

Workflows are described as saved markdown files defining a repeatable series of steps to guide the Agent through tasks across sessions — directly matching the capability of repeatable, named multi-step task sequences.

An Implementation Plan artifact details technical revisions needed, but the capability requires a reviewable plan produced *before* the agent edits anything (plan-then-execute gating). The feature description does not indicate the agent withholds edits pending plan approval — it's an artifact, not an execution gate.

Verification loops describe running test commands after changes and iterating, but the capability requires a verifiable 'done' condition set by the user that the agent loops on autonomously until met. The feature is about test-driven iteration, not user-defined goal-directed autonomous looping — adjacent but not the same.
Worktree Support explicitly has agents operate in dedicated Git worktrees to isolate parallel work within a project, directly realizing parallel isolated workspaces.
Agent Runtime is described as a Google Cloud-hosted, fully managed auto-scaling runtime that executes deployed ADK agents in the cloud — directly realizing the cloud execution environment capability.
Background Tasks are explicitly described as tracked, long-running operations run asynchronously without blocking the primary agent — directly realizing the background/async execution capability.
Scheduled Tasks allow users to schedule messages to agents with repeatable time-based triggers, directly realizing scheduled/triggered runs.
Terminal Sandboxing provides kernel-level/native OS isolation for terminal commands executed by the Agent in a restricted environment, directly realizing sandboxed execution.

The Fine-Grained Permissions Engine represents every sensitive agent operation as a permission, enabling approve/deny control over what the agent is allowed to do — directly realizing tool permission controls.
Independent Network Access Control explicitly controls network connectivity for sandboxed commands independently of filesystem restrictions, directly realizing network/egress control.

Building a Docker image from source (containerization) is not the same as dev-container integration, which means running the agent inside a defined dev container runtime (e.g., devcontainer.json). Building images is a precursor or adjacent concept, not the integration surface itself.
Agent activity logging provides structured, configurable logging of agent behavior using standard library facilities and OpenTelemetry — directly realizing audit logging/telemetry.

Quota Tracking monitors AI credit quota and consumption, which is closer to credit/quota management than cost and token-spend tracking with capping. It is adjacent but does not specifically realize tracking and capping token spend.

The /fork command clones conversation history into a new independent session, which is session branching/forking — not checkpointing or rewinding/undoing agent changes. This is the resume/fork capability, not checkpoint-rewind.
Resuming sessions allows users to return to prior conversations to continue an implementation or refine an existing solution, directly realizing the ability to resume a previous session.

An optional bundled web front-end is one specific access surface (web UI), not the capability of defining or enumerating access surfaces broadly (terminal, IDE, web, cloud, mobile). A single surface instance does not realize the general access-surfaces capability.
